reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
### Summary:
This paper introduces a Siamese voxel-to-Bird's Eye View (BEV) tracker designed for 3D object tracking in sparse point clouds. The framework employs a Siamese network to extract features from template and search areas, embeds template features into potential objects via global-local correlation learning, and enhances discriminability through shape-aware feature learning that generates dense point clouds from sparse inputs. The core innovation lies in decomposing 3D center regression into 2D BEV center localization (via voxel max-pooling) and z-axis prediction, eliminating proposal generation. The method claims state-of-the-art performance on KITTI and nuScenes datasets.  

---

### Main Review:  

#### 1. **Clarity and Scope Ambiguity**  
The paper is generally well-structured, with clear technical descriptions of the Siamese shape-aware feature learning and voxel-to-BEV localization modules (Sec. 3). However, **the application scope is ambiguously framed**. While the title and abstract position the work as a "3D tracker," the methodology (e.g., template-search matching per frame) and experiments (single-object metrics) align with *single* object tracking (SOT). Crucially, **tracking fundamentals**—trajectory management, occlusion handling, or identity persistence across frames—are never addressed. For instance, the testing protocol (Sec. 4.1) fuses the first GT box and previous result for template initialization but does not clarify how track identity is maintained during occlusions or re-entries. This conflation of detection (frame-wise localization) and tracking (multi-frame association) undermines the claimed contribution. The KITTI evaluation further confuses matters: SOT metrics (Success/Precision) are used, yet KITTI is a multi-object tracking (MOT) benchmark. Without MOT metrics (e.g., MOTA, HOTA) or comparisons to MOT baselines, the tracker's practical utility is unverified.  

#### 2. **Inadequate Coverage of Related Work**  
The literature review focuses narrowly on 3D SOT methods (SC3D, P2B, BAT) but **omits seminal MOT works** that define standard practices and metrics. For example:  
- **3D MOT frameworks** like *EagerMOT* (Kim et al., ICRA 2021) fuse detector outputs with motion models for sensor-agnostic tracking, while *AB3DMOT* (Weng et al., IROS 2020) establishes Kalman-filter baselines for KITTI/NuScenes.  
- **Large-scale datasets** (Waymo, JRDB) and metrics (MOTA, IDF1) critical for tracking robustness are unmentioned.  
- **2D MOT advancements** (e.g., FairMOT, CenterTrack) that inspire 3D designs are overlooked.  
This narrow scope misrepresents the tracking landscape. The claim that "most 3D MOT systems follow 2D schemes" (Sec. 2) ignores innovations like probabilistic data association (*Chiu et al., arXiv 2020*) or graph-based methods (*Weng et al., IROS 2020*). Such omissions weaken the paper’s contextual grounding.  

#### 3. **Limited Theoretical Contribution**  
The method integrates established techniques without significant innovation:  
- **Template embedding** (Sec. 3.1.1) adapts correlation learning from 2D Siamese trackers (e.g., SiamFC).  
- **Shape completion** (Sec. 3.1.2) mirrors SC3D but adds a gate mechanism for background suppression.  
- **BEV-based center regression** (Sec. 3.2) extends anchor-free detectors (e.g., AFDet) to tracking.  
While the *combination* of these ideas is novel, the paper lacks:  
  - **Ablations** proving the necessity of each component (e.g., impact of gate mechanism in sparse scenes).  
  - **Theoretical insights** into why BEV regression outperforms point/voxel proposals under sparsity.  
This engineering-centric approach is acceptable for application papers but requires exhaustive validation against diverse baselines—which is absent (see Point 4).  

#### 4. **Insufficient Experimental Validation**  
**Datasets and Metrics**:  
- Evaluations on KITTI use non-standard splits (scenes 19–20 for testing) rather than the official test set, preventing leaderboard comparison. MOT metrics (MOTA, IDs) are unused, making claims of "tracking" superiority unsubstantiated.  
- nuScenes validation uses SOT metrics, but the dataset is designed for MOT. Generalization tests ignore motion forecasting or occlusion-heavy sequences.  
**Baselines**:  
- Only SOT methods (SC3D, P2B, BAT) are compared. **3D MOT state-of-the-art** (e.g., *EagerMOT*, *CenterPoint*) and **strong detection baselines** (e.g., PointRCNN) are missing.  
**Scope Limitations**:  
- Tests on rigid objects (cars, vans) dominate; **non-rigid objects** (pedestrians, cyclists) are under-evaluated (Fig. 4 visualizations focus on cars).  
- The "sparse scenes" analysis (Tab. 2) uses ad-hoc point thresholds without justifying their relationship to real-world LiDAR degradation (e.g., weather, range).  

#### 5. **Ethical and Societal Impact**  
The authors omit this discussion. 3D tracking enables critical applications (autonomous driving, robotics) but risks misuse in surveillance. Privacy implications of tracking pedestrians/cyclists in public datasets (KITTI/nuScenes) warrant consideration.  

---

### Strengths:  
- The voxel-to-BEV target localization elegantly bypasses proposal generation, improving efficiency in sparse clouds.  
- Shape-aware learning via Chamfer loss (Eq. 6) effectively densifies point distributions.  
- Ablations on template generation (Tab. 5) validate design robustness.  

---

### Weaknesses:  
1. **Misalignment with tracking**: The framework is a detection-localization loop, not a tracker.  
2. **Narrow comparisons**: Omits 3D MOT leaders and detection baselines.  
3. **Incomplete evaluation**: Lacks standard metrics, occlusion tests, and real-world sparsity benchmarks.  
4. **Reproducibility**: Code is provided, but KITTI test-set results and training details for nuScenes are vague.  

---

### Recommendations:  
- Reframe the work as "3D single object detection" or add multi-object association modules.  
- Compare against MOTA-driven trackers on KITTI/NuScenes test sets.  
- Include ablations on shape completion efficacy and BEV vs. voxel trade-offs.  
- Discuss failure modes (e.g., rapid motion, dense crowds).  

---

### Rating:  
**4: Ok but not good enough - rejection**  
While technically sound, the paper overstates its tracking contribution, lacks benchmarking against relevant state-of-the-art, and uses non-standard evaluation. The experiments do not sufficiently justify scalability to real-world MOT scenarios.

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

reviews.text:
### Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a rigorous theoretical analysis of the generalization error of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) in two distinct scenarios: (i) when test tasks recur from the training set, and (ii) when test tasks are unseen. Under strong convexity assumptions and a single-step SGD adaptation, the authors derive novel generalization bounds. For recurring tasks, they propose a new stability notion that captures the role of per-task sample size (\(n\)), yielding a tighter \(O(1/mn)\) bound compared to prior work. For unseen tasks, they introduce a bound based on the total variation (TV) distance between task distributions, providing a fresh perspective on task transferability.  

**Originality & Significance:**  
The paper makes meaningful contributions to meta-learning theory. The proposed \((γ, K)\)-uniform stability (Definition 3) is a significant advancement over existing definitions (e.g., [28]), as it explicitly incorporates the number of samples per task (\(n\)) into the generalization bound. This refinement is practically relevant, as tasks in domains like medical imaging or robotics often have limited data (small \(n\)), and the bound \(O(1/mn)\) more accurately reflects the sample efficiency gains from multi-task learning. The TV distance analysis for unseen tasks (Theorem 3) is equally novel, formalizing the intuition that task similarity governs transfer performance. The coupling technique used here to eliminate dependence on \(K\) (test-time adaptation samples) is elegant and strengthens the result. These theoretical advances offer a more nuanced understanding of MAML’s generalization behavior.  

**Quality:**  
The analysis is technically sound but faces inherent limitations due to its assumptions:  
1.  **Strong convexity (Assumption 1):** This is the primary weakness. MAML is predominantly applied to non-convex neural networks. While Remark 2 justifies the assumption by noting the meta-objective becomes non-convex otherwise, the gap between theory and practice remains substantial. For example, the analysis cannot explain MAML’s performance on standard few-shot image benchmarks (e.g., Mini-ImageNet), where losses are highly non-convex. The authors correctly identify this but could further discuss potential pathways for extending the analysis (e.g., via Polyak-Łojasiewicz conditions) or empirical validation of the theoretical insights in convex proxy tasks.  
2.  **Single-step SGD adaptation:** The restriction to one adaptation step (Eq. 1, Algorithm 1) simplifies the analysis but limits applicability. Most empirical MAML variants use multiple steps (e.g., [1, 9]). The challenge lies in the rapid growth of higher-order derivatives in the meta-gradient (Eq. 5) with more steps, which complicates stability analysis. Explicitly discussing this trade-off and the open problem of multi-step generalization would strengthen the paper.  
3.  **Boundedness (Assumption 3):** While common in generalization analysis, this may not hold for unbounded losses like cross-entropy in high-capacity models. The impact of this assumption on the TV distance bound (Theorem 3) warrants brief discussion.  

The paper is well-structured, with clear proofs and logical progression. The stability-to-generalization link (Theorem 1) is well-established, and the use of task-averaged iterates (\(\bar{w}_T\)) in Proposition 2 is a practical choice given their superior convergence. The discussion on weight adjustments for non-uniform task distributions (Remark 3) is a valuable practical insight.  

**Typos/Minor Issues (Corrections):**  
-   Assumption 1(ii): "unbounded" → **bounded**.  
-   Line 196: "from [1]" → **from [13, 24]** (Lemma 1 attribution).  
-   Line 218: "deferentially private" → **differentially private**.  
-   Lines 286-287: "k" → **K** (consistency with notation).  
-   Section 3.3 Title: "Generalization to an unseen Task" → **Generalization to an Unseen Task**.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The paper makes tangible theoretical contributions through its novel stability definition and TV distance analysis, offering improved bounds and deeper insights into MAML’s generalization. However, the strong convexity and single-step adaptation assumptions restrict the direct applicability of the results to common deep learning scenarios. While the limitations are acknowledged, they remain significant. The work is a solid theoretical advance suitable for publication, provided the community recognizes its scope.

reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No limitation or potential negative societal impact is shown.

On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.

Main Review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. 1. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Expanded Review:**

**Summary:**  
This paper provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of generalization bounds for Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) under two scenarios: (1) recurring tasks (where the test task is sampled from the training distribution) and (2) unseen tasks (where the test task is entirely novel). For recurring tasks, the authors derive a generalization bound of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objectives, highlighting the interplay between the number of tasks ($m$) and samples per task ($n$). For unseen tasks, they introduce a bound dependent on the total variation distance between the new task’s distribution and the training tasks’ distributions. The analysis leverages a novel stability definition tailored to meta-learning, differing from prior work by explicitly capturing dependencies on both $m$ and $n$. While the paper is purely theoretical, the results contribute meaningful insights into the factors governing MAML’s generalization.

**Main Review:**  
Overall, this is a strong theoretical contribution that advances our understanding of MAML’s generalization properties. The authors’ stability-based approach is well-motivated, and the separation of recurring versus unseen task analyses addresses distinct practical considerations. Below, I elaborate on the strengths and address technical concerns raised in the original review.

**Strengths:**  
1. **Novel Stability Definition:** The proposed $(γ, K)$-uniform stability (Definition 3) crucially departs from prior definitions (e.g., [28]) by perturbing $K$ samples per task rather than entire task datasets. This enables finer-grained control over the dependence on $n$, leading to tighter bounds (e.g., $O(1/mn)$ vs. $O(1/m)$ in [28]). The connection to generalization error (Theorem 1) is clearly established, solidifying the framework’s utility.  

2. **Practical Implications for Unseen Tasks:** The total variation distance-based bound (Theorem 3) formalizes an intuitive relationship between task similarity and generalization. The coupling technique used to eliminate dependence on $K$ in Equation 11 is particularly elegant, ensuring the bound remains practical even for few-shot adaptation ($K \ll n$). Corollary 2 further clarifies how task-weighted training could improve generalization, suggesting actionable insights for practitioners.  

3. **Integration of Optimization and Generalization:** The decomposition of excess loss into training and generalization errors (Section 3.1–3.2) aligns with classical statistical learning theory while adapting it to the meta-learning setting. Proposition 1’s explicit characterization of MAML’s training error under strongly convex objectives complements existing convergence analyses (e.g., [13,15]).

**Technical Concerns:**  
1. **Non-Convex Challenges and Stability:** The authors note that their stability analysis is limited to convex settings due to algorithmic stability’s reliance on small learning rates in non-convex regimes (Remark 2, Appendix G). This limitation is well-acknowledged, but further discussion would strengthen the paper. For instance, in non-convex settings, even with uniform stability, bounds often scale as $O(1/\sqrt{T})$ due to the lack of exponential error decay [Hardt et al., 2016]. In MAML, the meta-objective’s non-convexity (stemming from nested gradients) exacerbates this issue, making meaningful generalization bounds difficult. The authors’ decision to focus on strongly convex objectives is pragmatic, as it avoids these complexities while delivering interpretable results.  

2. **Connection to Bilevel Optimization:** The analysis assumes a single-level optimization perspective (Equation 4), differing from the bilevel formulation common in meta-learning (e.g., [Ji et al., 2020]). While this simplification aids tractability, it sidesteps challenges inherent to bilevel frameworks, such as nested gradient estimation and equilibrium points. Recent work on bilevel stability (e.g., [Grazzi et al., 2021]) suggests that extensions to bilevel MAML would require bounding stability *both* in the inner-task adaptation and meta-update steps—a non-trivial endeavor. The authors’ current analysis lays groundwork for such extensions but does not address them directly. Including a discussion of these distinctions (e.g., in Section 5) would better position the work within the broader meta-learning literature.  

**Additional Observations:**  
- **Empirical Validation:** While the paper is theoretical, a brief discussion of how the bounds align with empirical observations (e.g., the correlation between task coherence and generalization in [29]) could bridge theory and practice. For instance, the $O(1/mn)$ recurring task bound implies that increasing tasks or samples per task equally improves generalization, a hypothesis testable via controlled experiments.  
- **Assumption Trade-offs:** The strong convexity requirement (Assumption 1) enables clean bounds but limits applicability to settings like deep learning. The authors might briefly comment on whether relaxed assumptions (e.g., PL-inequality) could preserve similar rates, as suggested in Remark 2.  

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**  
The authors appropriately note that extending results to non-convex settings remains open. Additionally, the reliance on total variation distance for unseen tasks may be restrictive in high-dimensional spaces, where Wasserstein metrics are more informative. While the theoretical focus poses no direct societal risks, practitioners applying these insights should consider ethical implications of meta-learning systems, particularly when task distributions encode biases.

**Decision:**  
This paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by deriving rigorous generalization bounds for MAML, addressing both recurring and unseen tasks. While the analysis is limited to convex objectives, the results are novel and practically relevant. The concerns raised are outweighed by the clarity and utility of the theoretical framework.  

**Rating:**  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a rigorous theoretical analysis of generalization properties for Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML), addressing both recurring and unseen tasks. For recurring tasks (test task drawn from training tasks), the authors derive an $O(1/mn)$ generalization bound under strong convexity assumptions, leveraging a novel $(\gamma, K)$-uniform stability framework. For unseen tasks, they bound generalization error via the total variation distance between the unseen task’s distribution and training task distributions. While the theoretical contributions are technically sound, experimental validation is limited to a synthetic regression task in supplementary materials, leaving broader applicability unverified.  

**Pros (Expanded):**  
1.  **Clarity and Structure:** The paper is exceptionally well-organized. Notations (e.g., $S^{\text{in}}_i$, $S^{\text{out}}_i$, $\hat{F}$, $F_i$), assumptions (strong convexity, smoothness, bounded gradients), and definitions (e.g., $(\gamma, K)$-uniform stability in Def. 3, TV distance in Def. 4) are introduced precisely. The separation of analyses for training error (Prop. 1), recurring task generalization (Thm. 1, 2, Prop. 2, Cor. 1), and unseen task generalization (Thm. 3, Cor. 2) enhances readability. The connection between algorithmic stability and generalization (Thm. 1) is clearly established for the meta-learning setting.  
2.  **Theoretical Significance:** Analyzing MAML’s generalization, particularly for unseen tasks, addresses a critical gap. The $O(1/mn)$ bound for recurring tasks explicitly quantifies the benefit of *both* more tasks ($m$) and more samples *per task* ($n$), a refinement over prior bounds like the $O(1/\sqrt{m})$ in [28] that obscured the role of $n$. This theoretically justifies the importance of sufficient intra-task data during meta-training. The use of maximal coupling (Hollander, 2012) to eliminate dependence on the adaptation step size $K$ in the unseen task bound (Thm. 3, Eq. 10) is a clever and non-trivial technical contribution.  
3.  **Novel Stability Framework:** The proposed $(\gamma, K)$-uniform stability (Def. 3) is a key innovation. By explicitly modeling the perturbation of $K$ points in $S^{\text{in}}_i$ (the adaptation batch) and one point in $S^{\text{out}}_i$ (the loss evaluation set), it directly captures the structure of the MAML objective (Eq. 3) and enables the tight $O(1/mn)$ dependence. This contrasts effectively with coarser stability notions (e.g., [28]) that change entire tasks ($S_i$), failing to isolate the impact of $n$.  

**Cons (Expanded):**  
1.  **Bound Tightness and Surprise Factor:**  
    *   While the $O(1/mn)$ dependence on the *total* number of samples is information-theoretically optimal for strongly convex problems, the tightness concerning other parameters remains unclear. The bound scales linearly with $K$ (Cor. 1: $O((1 + \alpha L K)/mn\mu)$), but it is unknown if this is unavoidable or an artifact of the analysis. Does the true error degrade linearly with larger adaptation steps $K$? A lower bound construction or discussion would strengthen this result.  
    *   The bounds, though technically refined, essentially confirm expected scaling laws (error decreases with more data/tasks). They lack surprising insights into the *unique* dynamics of MAML’s bi-level optimization. For instance, how does the interplay between the meta-update step size $\beta_t$ and the adaptation step size $\alpha$ influence generalization? Does the structure of the Hessian-term in the MAML gradient (Eq. 5, 6) induce implicit regularization affecting generalization beyond what stability captures? Comparisons like the support/query split analysis in [Chen et al., 2020] offered more MAML-specific insights.  
2.  **Insufficient Empirical Validation:**  
    *   **Scope and Robustness:** The single synthetic linear regression experiment (Supp.) is inadequate. The high variance in the reported learning curves suggests insufficient averaging (e.g., over task distributions or algorithm seeds). Claims about $m$ and $n$ scaling need validation on standard, non-synthetic few-shot benchmarks (e.g., miniImageNet, CIFAR-FS) using realistic neural architectures.  
    *   **Missing Critical Experiments:**  
        -   **Impact of $K$:** The theory predicts generalization error for recurring tasks grows with $K$ (Cor. 1). An experiment varying $K$ (test adaptation batch size) while fixing $m, n$ is essential to verify this linear dependence.  
        -   **TV Distance Validation:** The core insight for unseen tasks (Thm. 3, Cor. 2) is that generalization depends on the TV distance $D(p_{m+1}, \{p_i\})$. This *must* be tested. Construct tasks (e.g., image datasets) with controlled TV distances (e.g., by mixing classes or adding noise) and measure if the test error on $p_{m+1}$ correlates with $D(p_{m+1}, \{p_i\})$ as predicted. Does this metric reliably predict generalization difficulty? This could be highly impactful.  
        -   **Real-world Application:** Demonstrating the bounds hold, even qualitatively, on a standard few-shot *classification* task would significantly bolster relevance.  
3.  **Novelty of Derivations:**  
    *   The training error analysis (Prop. 1) directly adapts standard SGD convergence results ([30-32]) to the strongly convex meta-loss $\hat{F}$, leveraging its established properties (smoothness, strong convexity via Lemma 1 from [13, 24]). While competently executed, the core challenges here stem from prior work.  
    *   The stability analysis (Thm. 2) builds elegantly upon the classic framework of [17, 18], but the significant challenge overcome is tailoring the stability definition (Def. 3) and its proof to the intricate structure of the MAML objective (Eq. 3, involving nested expectations and batches). This adaptation is non-trivial but relies heavily on the foundation of uniform stability arguments. The maximal coupling technique (Thm. 3) is a powerful import from probability theory applied effectively here.  

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**  
The analysis is restricted to strongly convex loss functions, limiting direct applicability to non-convex settings like deep neural networks commonly used with MAML. Extensions to convex but non-strongly convex losses are noted (Remark 2) but not fully developed. Societal impacts are not discussed, but standard considerations around ML fairness and bias in meta-learning systems would apply.  

**Decision Justification:**  
This paper makes solid theoretical contributions: a novel $(\gamma, K)$-stability framework yielding a tight $O(1/mn)$ bound for recurring tasks and a clever TV distance bound for unseen tasks via maximal coupling. However, the **lack of convincing empirical support** significantly undermines the impact. The single synthetic experiment fails to validate the theory under practical conditions or explore critical dependencies ($K$, TV distance). Furthermore, while technically sound, the core stability and training error analyses build substantially on established frameworks, and the bounds, though refined, lack highly surprising insights specific to MAML's bi-level structure. Therefore, while marginally above threshold due to the theoretical rigor and novel stability definition, the work falls short of a strong accept.  

**Recommendation:**  
**Weak Reject (6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold)**. The theoretical contributions are valuable but require significantly stronger empirical validation and exploration of the bounds' implications (e.g., TV distance as a task selection criterion) to reach their full potential. Addressing the experimental limitations in future work is crucial.  

**Rating:**  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.

Main Review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.
reviews.main_review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The assumption of strong convexity and smoothness is very strong and does not hold for most neural networks. The authors have not fully addressed such limitation.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper provides a generalization analysis of MAML. Based on a new definition of stability of meta-learning, it characterizes the generalization error on seen and unseen tasks.

Main Review:
Please address the following concerns:
- The results in Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 both show that the generalization error increases for a larger K (the number of shots). This conflicts with the experiment results in MAML or other meta-learning papers. So I have a major concern about whether the bound is vacuous. Moreover, it would be better to empirically verify the effect of K. 
- The theoretic results of Theorem 2, i.e., the generalization error of seen tasks, are not empirically studied. It would also better to provide some simple experiments to verify it.
- The equation in line 569 seems not so straightforward. The loss of the i-th task is not only affected by the replacement of K data points in $S_i^n$, but also affected by the replacement of data points in $S_j^n (j \neq i)$. So I wonder whether expectations of $w_S$ and $w_\tilde{S}$ are the same.

****************************************************After Rebuttal**********************************************
- While I am satisfied with the response to the assumption of strong convexity and smoothness, I am still not convinced by the response to my major concern regarding $K$. 
     - When $K$ is small which is usually the case in meta-learning, this bound is as loose as SGD. In this case, why do we need meta-learning?
     - Only when $K$ is as large as possible (Appendix E), the error decreases as $K$ increases. In fact, according to the bound, if the total number of samples across all tasks is $1M$, $K$ has to be even as large as $\sim10,000$, which is rarely practical in meta-learning.

If the latest discussion by the authors can be included, I tend to increase the score to "weakly accept".

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The assumption of strong convexity and smoothness is very strong and does not hold for most neural networks. The authors have not fully addressed such limitation.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
**Summary:**  
This paper provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of the generalization properties of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML). The authors introduce a novel stability definition for meta-learning algorithms and derive generalization bounds for two scenarios: (1) recurring tasks (i.e., test tasks observed during training) and (2) unseen tasks. For recurring tasks, they prove an expected excess population loss bound of \(O(1/mn)\) under strong convexity and smoothness assumptions. For unseen tasks, they show the generalization error scales with the total variation distance between the unseen task's distribution and the training tasks' distributions. The analysis leverages connections between algorithmic stability and generalization, extending classical results to the meta-learning setting.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Novel Stability Definition:** The proposed \(( \gamma, K )\)-uniform stability (Definition 3) effectively captures the sensitivity of MAML to perturbations in both inner-loop (\(K\) samples) and outer-loop (single sample) data units. This is a meaningful extension of classical uniform stability to meta-learning.  
2. **Tighter Bounds:** For recurring tasks, the bound \(O(1/mn)\) improves upon prior work (e.g., \(O(1/\sqrt{m})\) in [28]) by explicitly accounting for the number of samples per task (\(n\)). This refinement highlights the interplay between task diversity (\(m\)) and data abundance (\(n\)).  
3. **Unseen Task Generalization:** Theorem 3 provides a principled characterization of how distributional shift (via TV distance) affects generalization to new tasks. The coupling technique used to decouple \(K\) from the bound is elegant and technically sound (Appendix F).  
4. **Clear Technical Execution:** The paper is well-structured, with key assumptions (e.g., strong convexity, smoothness) cleanly motivating the theoretical results. The decomposition of excess loss into training and generalization errors (Section 2) is particularly instructive.  

**Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1. **Conflict between Theory and Practice for \(K\):**  
   * **Original Critique:** Theorem 2 and Corollary 2 indicate the generalization error *increases* with the number of adaptation shots \(K\) (i.e., \(O(K)\) dependence). This contradicts empirical evidence from MAML and related meta-learning work, where performance typically *improves* with larger \(K\).  
   * **Expansion:** This discrepancy raises concerns about the bound's practicality. The analysis assumes \(K\) affects the Lipschitz constant of the meta-objective (via Lemma 1), leading to the \(O(K)\) term. While valid under the stated assumptions, this suggests the bound may be loose or vacuous in the small-\(K\) regime central to few-shot learning. For example, if \(K\) is small (e.g., 1-5 shots as is typical), the \(O(1/mn)\) term dominates, but the \(O(K)\) term still grows counterfactually. The authors should address whether this reflects a true limitation of MAML under strong convexity or an artifact of the analysis. *Empirical validation on simple synthetic tasks (e.g., linear regression) is needed to test the \(K\)-dependence claim.*  
   * **Post-Rebuttal Concern:** The rebuttal notes the bound is tight only for small \(K\), but this is precisely where meta-learning is most relevant. For \(K\) large enough to make the bound decrease (e.g., \(K \sim 10,000\) for \(mn \sim 1e6\)), MAML reduces to standard transfer learning, negating its core motivation. This undermines the theoretical contribution for practical few-shot settings.  

2. **Lack of Empirical Validation:**  
   * **Original Critique:** The theoretical claims, especially the \(O(1/mn)\) decay for recurring tasks (Theorem 2) and the TV distance dependence for unseen tasks (Theorem 3), lack empirical support.  
   * **Expansion:** Simple experiments could strengthen the paper significantly. For instance:  
     - Verify the \(1/mn\) scaling by measuring test loss versus \(m\) and \(n\) on synthetic tasks with strongly convex losses (e.g., regularized logistic regression).  
     - Correlate the generalization gap on unseen tasks with the empirical TV distance between their data distributions and training tasks. The current reliance on synthetic results in Appendix E is insufficient; main-text experiments are needed to ground the theory.  

3. **Proof Assumption in Equation (Line 569):**  
   * **Original Critique:** The stability analysis (Appendix D) assumes that replacing \(K\) points in \(S_i^{in}\) for task \(i\) does not affect the model’s expectation via other tasks \(j \neq i\), potentially overlooking cross-task dependencies.  
   * **Expansion:** While the authors use the symmetry of MAML and the independence of task data (Assumption 2), it is unclear how the coupling argument in Theorem 3 (Appendix F) generalizes when *multiple* tasks experience perturbations. The proof of Lemma 6 (Appendix D.3) should explicitly address whether the expectation \(\mathbb{E}[w_S]\) remains stable under such perturbations, as cross-task interactions could propagate instability.  

4. **Strong Assumptions Limit Applicability:**  
   * **Original Critique:** Strong convexity, smoothness, and bounded gradients (Assumption 1) rarely hold for deep neural networks used in practical MAML implementations.  
   * **Expansion:** While these assumptions enable tractable analysis, they significantly restrict the practical relevance of the results. For example, the \(\mu\)-strong convexity requirement (A1(i)) is incompatible with non-convex losses like cross-entropy. The authors acknowledge this in Section 4 but should discuss *how severely* these assumptions might affect generalization in real-world settings. For instance, could the \(O(K)\) artifact in Theorem 2 stem from strong convexity?  

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**  
- **Theoretical Scope:** The analysis is restricted to strongly convex objectives. Extending to non-convex settings (e.g., DNNs) remains challenging, as noted in Section 4.  
- **Empirical Gaps:** The lack of experimental validation weakens the paper’s impact. Claims about \(K\)-dependence and TV distance remain hypothetical.  
- **Societal Impact:** While theoretical, this work could inform more sample-efficient meta-learning algorithms. No direct negative societal impacts are apparent, but practitioners should note the assumptions limit direct applicability to complex models.  

**Recommendation:**  
The paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by deriving novel generalization bounds for MAML via a well-motivated stability framework. However, the **conflict between theory and practice regarding \(K\)**, the **lack of empirical support**, and **residual concerns about proof techniques** significantly dampen enthusiasm. The authors’ rebuttal addressed some issues (e.g., clarifying \(K\)-dependence in small-shot regimes) but did not fully resolve the core tension.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The paper’s novel stability definition and improved bounds for recurring tasks are noteworthy, but the unresolved issues highlighted above prevent a higher score. Acceptance would hinge on: (1) stronger empirical validation, (2) a more nuanced discussion of the \(K\)-dependence paradox, and (3) clarification of cross-task dependencies in the stability analysis.

reviews.summary: The authors present generalization bounds for MAML when test tasks are the same as the ones seen during training (recurring tasks) or when they are different but sampled from the same distribution (unseen tasks). The main takeaways are a. for recurring tasks, the excess population loss is bounded by O(1/mn) with m the number of tasks and n the number of data points, and b. generalization to unseen tasks depends on the distance (total variation) between train and test task distributions.
reviews.main_review:
The results of the paper are novel and of interest to the multi-task/meta-learning community. In particular, the generalization result for recurring tasks (Th. 1 & 2) refines multi-tasks bounds for MAML when data is limited. This is important since limited data is often the main motivation for these types of meta-learning methods. While the approach to get those bounds builds on the stability of SGD, the proposed stability definition is tailored to MAML and lends itself to further study and extensions. The second result (Th. 3) directly targets transfer to new tasks, formalizing one of the main intuitions behind MAML: the closer the train and test tasks, the most effective the transfer. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first work directly assessing generalization for MAML when train and test task sets are disjoint.

It helps that the paper is clearly written and introduces all necessary background to MAML. For each theorem, assumptions are clearly stated and the results are discussed from both a theoretical and practical perspective. Although a bit dense, I enjoyed reading this paper.

The main limitation of the paper is convexity of the meta-learning loss — but similar publications also considered this setting. Specifically, I would like to see the assumptions on the fast-adaptation learning rate (alpha) relaxed, since they are rarely met in practice.

Finally, I wished the related work section were a bit more expansive. For example, issues of generalization were also considered in [1], and [2] discussed issues related to the number of samples per tasks. How does the present work differ?

References:

[1] "Global Convergence and Generalization Bound of Gradient-Based Meta-Learning with Deep Neural Nets", Wang et al., ArXiv 2020.

[2] "How to distribute data across tasks for meta-learning?", Cioba et al., ArXiv 2021.

reviews.text:
### Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper provides a rigorous theoretical analysis of generalization bounds for Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) in two distinct settings: (i) recurring tasks (test tasks identical to training tasks) and (ii) unseen tasks (test tasks drawn from distributions different from training tasks). The key contributions are:  
- For recurring tasks, the authors establish an *O(1/mn)* bound on the excess population loss, where *m* is the number of training tasks and *n* is the number of samples per task. This refines prior multi-task generalization bounds by explicitly accounting for per-task sample efficiency.  
- For unseen tasks, generalization error scales with the total variation (TV) distance between test-task distributions (*p_{m+1}*) and the average training distribution (*\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^m p_i*), formalizing the intuition that task similarity drives transferability.  
The analysis leverages a novel stability framework tailored to MAML's bilevel structure, addressing gaps in prior meta-learning generalization theory.  

**Strengths and Novelty:**  
The paper makes significant contributions to understanding MAML's generalization properties. The recurring-task bound (*O(1/mn)*) is particularly compelling as it highlights the dual role of task diversity (*m*) and per-task data (*n*)—an insight absent in prior works like [28], which only achieved *O(1/\sqrt{m})*. This refinement is enabled by the proposed *(γ, K)*-stability framework (Definition 3), which perturbs *K* samples in the inner-loop support set (*S_i^{in}*) and one sample in the query set (*S_i^{out}*), explicitly modeling MAML's adaptation mechanics. The proof technique for unseen tasks (Theorem 3) is equally innovative: by coupling task distributions and avoiding naive Lipschitz extensions, the authors sidestep a *K*-dependence in the TV distance bound, aligning better with practical few-shot settings where *K* is small.  

The paper is exceptionally well-structured, with clear technical exposition. For example, the decomposition of excess risk into training/generalization errors (Section 3.1–3.2) provides a lucid roadmap, and the discussion of stability (Section 3.2) elegantly bridges classical SGD analysis to meta-learning. The authors also thoughtfully contextualize their assumptions: Remark 2 acknowledges the strong convexity limitation but justifies it as essential for tractable stability analysis, while Appendix E hints at extensions to large-*K* regimes.  

**Limitations and Suggestions:**  
The convexity assumption (Assumption 1) and restrictive step-size constraints (*α ≤ \min\{1/(2L), \mu/(8ρG)\}*) remain the primary limitations. While the authors note these are common in theoretical work (e.g., [13, 24]), they rarely hold in modern deep learning deployments where MAML excels. For instance, the requirement for *μ*-strong convexity excludes overparameterized neural nets, and the small *α* contradicts empirical MAML implementations that use larger inner-loop learning rates. Relaxing these—possibly via Polyak-Łojasiewicz conditions or overparameterization analysis—would broaden applicability.  

The related work section could engage more deeply with contemporary studies. For example:  
- [1] (Wang et al., 2020) derives generalization bounds for nonconvex MAML but assumes task distributions are identical; contrasting this with the TV-distance framework for task-heterogeneity (Theorem 3) would highlight novelty.  
- [2] (Cioba et al., 2021) studies optimal per-task data allocation but focuses on optimization error; discussing how their distribution-aware sampling might interact with the *O(1/mn)* bound could enrich the synthesis.  

**Societal Impact:**  
The paper focuses on theoretical analysis and does not discuss societal implications. However, as MAML is widely applied in sensitive domains (e.g., healthcare), future extensions should address how distributional divergence (Theorem 3) might propagate biases when transferring between demographically skewed task distributions.  

**Decision:**  
This paper makes valuable theoretical advances in meta-learning generalization, particularly through its novel stability framework and distribution-aware bounds. The analysis is rigorous, and the results meaningfully advance our understanding of MAML's behavior in both recurring and unseen-task settings. Despite the unavoidable convexity assumptions, the insights are significant for the community. I recommend **acceptance (rating: 7)**.  

### References  
[1] Wang et al., "Global Convergence and Generalization Bound of Gradient-Based Meta-Learning with Deep Neural Nets", ArXiv 2020.  
[2] Cioba et al., "How to distribute data across tasks for meta-learning?", ArXiv 2021.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors present generalization bounds for MAML when test tasks are the same as the ones seen during training (recurring tasks) or when they are different but sampled from the same distribution (unseen tasks). The main takeaways are a. for recurring tasks, the excess population loss is bounded by O(1/mn) with m the number of tasks and n the number of data points, and b. generalization to unseen tasks depends on the distance (total variation) between train and test task distributions.

Main Review:
The results of the paper are novel and of interest to the multi-task/meta-learning community. In particular, the generalization result for recurring tasks (Th. 1 & 2) refines multi-tasks bounds for MAML when data is limited. This is important since limited data is often the main motivation for these types of meta-learning methods. While the approach to get those bounds builds on the stability of SGD, the proposed stability definition is tailored to MAML and lends itself to further study and extensions. The second result (Th. 3) directly targets transfer to new tasks, formalizing one of the main intuitions behind MAML: the closer the train and test tasks, the most effective the transfer. To the best of my knowledge, this is the first work directly assessing generalization for MAML when train and test task sets are disjoint.

It helps that the paper is clearly written and introduces all necessary background to MAML. For each theorem, assumptions are clearly stated and the results are discussed from both a theoretical and practical perspective. Although a bit dense, I enjoyed reading this paper.

The main limitation of the paper is convexity of the meta-learning loss — but similar publications also considered this setting. Specifically, I would like to see the assumptions on the fast-adaptation learning rate (alpha) relaxed, since they are rarely met in practice.

Finally, I wished the related work section were a bit more expansive. For example, issues of generalization were also considered in [1], and [2] discussed issues related to the number of samples per tasks. How does the present work differ?

References:

[1] "Global Convergence and Generalization Bound of Gradient-Based Meta-Learning with Deep Neural Nets", Wang et al., ArXiv 2020.

[2] "How to distribute data across tasks for meta-learning?", Cioba et al., ArXiv 2021.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitations are clearly stated, societal impact is not mentioned.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization properties of MAML in the strongly convex setting. It's assumed that there are $m$ training tasks, each with $n$ samples, and the inner loop adaptation is one step of SGD using $K$ samples. It's proved that if the new task is a uniformly random task from one of the $m$ training tasks, MAML incurs a generalization error of $O(\frac{K}{mn})$; if the new task is an unseen task, the error depends on the TV distance between the new task distribution and all the training task distributions as well as their average. The analysis is based on a modified version of algorithmic stability tailored to the MAML setup.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: The contribution of this paper is new. The paper obtains an $O(1/mn)$ bound in the strongly convex setting, while the previous result in [25] is $O(1/m)$ (though [25] covers non-convex losses). The proof technique is based on algorithmic stability but also needs nontrivial modification.

Quality: The paper is technically sound. The results are clearly stated and discussed.

Clarity: The paper is clearly written and well organized.

Significance:
- I think the most interesting result is the $O(1/mn)$ error bound for "in-task generalization", i.e., generalizing to a random training task. The previous bound for MAML is only $O(1/n)$ in [25]. To obtain the $O(1/mn)$ bound, a new type of stability (Definition 3) is required, which is non-trivial.
- However, I believe the result of generalization to an unseen task (Theorem 3) is very loose and does not capture the actual interesting aspects of meta-learning. Essentially, for this bound to be small it requires the new task distribution to be very close in TV distance to all the training tasks as well as their average. However, this is almost never the case. For example, suppose that all the tasks are drawn i.i.d. from a meta-distribution over tasks (which is usually true in a lot of few-shot learning benchmarks). Then $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_{m+1}$ could all be very different from each other, but we can still expect MAML to generalize well to $p_{m+1}$, since it's a task drawn from the same meta-distribution. The bound provided in the current paper dramatically fails to capture this.
- The strong convexity is certainly very restrictive, and it's noted that the technique used in the paper cannot generalize beyond the convex case. That said, this is understandable and acceptable as a first step for theoretical purpose. 

Other questions:
- The paper only considers 1-step SGD adaptation in the inner loop. Is there any hope to generalize the result to multiple steps?
- It seems that a somewhat non-standard data splitting procedure is used, i.e. first splitting the data into $S_i^{in}$ and $S_i^{out}$ each with size $n$, and then the samples used for inner and outer loops are sampled from these two subsets. How important is this in the theoretical analysis and is it unavoidable?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As noted in the main review, I believe the generalization result to an unseen task is very loose and can be misleading. A discussion of this is needed.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
