reviews.summary: This paper uses Huang and Jiang (2020) to talk about gradient-based meta learning in reinforcement learning using the language of off-policy evaluation. The logical connection behind those concepts stands from the fact that policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning are in fact instances of the likelihood ratio derivative estimator, which itself stems from important sampling. Proposition 3.2 and 3.3 are obtained from Huang and Jiang. Proposition 3.1 tries to extend their results to talk about higher order derivatives. The author's finally propose to use ideas from Taylor policy optimization from Tang et al. (2020) as a variance reduction method within the doubly robust policy gradient framework of Huang and Jiang.
reviews.main_review:
In my opinion the paper doesn't go deep enough into the individual ideas being put forward. The doubly robust estimator of Huang and Jiang is at the core of this paper.  In the specific context of meta learning, what does it allow us to say or do that we couldn't achieve before? Why is doubly robust necessary? The doubly robust being a variance reduction technique why this VRT in particular and not another one, in the meta learning context? Regarding the Taylor approximation, why is it related to the doubly robust estimator? The paper does not do a convincing job at showing that these two components strongly depend on each other: conceptually, or experimentally. Overall, the paper lacks cohesion and depth.

On line 83 the notation suggests that the expectation is not taken over x and only over the task g. While technically correct, you don't really have to write the objective as an expectation over the value function. Your objective should simply be the expected discounted or on discounted return, written plainly as a sum of rewards.

In equation 2, I would suggest using a different notation when you're writing nabla of theta prime. Theta prime should be a function, but the way that it's written suggests that it's a variable instead.

Another suggestion for notation would be to write "v of theta" (instead of a superscript) in equation 2. This way it would be clearer how you have to apply the chain rule later on.

Reading more carefully on line 92 and equation 1 now I realize that you assume a fixed initial distribution starting from x0. This is confusing because on line 83 you simply write v of x and it looks like x should be a random variable: your initial state. Perhaps you should state explicitly that you assume (without loss of generality) a given initial state.

On line 92, the expression the "Hessian of the value function" is misleading because really the Hessian that you're taking is that of the expected return objective, not that of the value function.

I think that the unnumbered equation below line 106 is redundant: it simply says that the two quantities are unbiased estimators. Words suffice here. Instead, I would suggest that you explain the nature of the issue. The correlation problem that you're describing comes from taking the expectation over a product of two random variables. The fact that it's not simply the product of the individual expectations and that here is also a covariance term.

I'm confused by proposition 3.1 especially around line 137 when you introduce a new notation for the estimator nabla hat v. What you have in this proposition should be something about a derivative estimator: score function/REINFORCE, reparametrization. Now you don't seem to be defining how you obtain this nabla theta m  v hat, and in that sense proposition 3.1 does not quite stand on its own.

On line 171 you allude to the fact that the variance of policy learning is essentially due to having a large number of importance sampling terms. While this is a reasonable intuition to have, note that we can show formally that PDIS-type estimators need not have larger variance than let's say stationary or marginal IS.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitation of the framework per se are not quite explored. The authors talk about what can be said within that framework (dice) but not quite what it cannot say. Experimentally the paper looks at the performance of the combination of doubly robust plus Taylor approximation as an algorithm of its own. It does not quite try to understand what the doubly robust formulation allows us to address specifically in meta learning. It would be interesting to gain more insights into the value of the proposed method by taking a more ablative approach. Since the authors pitch a framework as the main contribution, performance results matter much less than experimental insights at the conceptual level.  I think that the paper could be restructured around the algorithm and to de-emphasize the framework part. To go all in on the algorithm (which might be how things happened chronologically anyways).

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper uses Huang and Jiang (2020) to talk about gradient-based meta learning in reinforcement learning using the language of off-policy evaluation. The logical connection behind those concepts stands from the fact that policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning are in fact instances of the likelihood ratio derivative estimator, which itself stems from important sampling. Proposition 3.2 and 3.3 are obtained from Huang and Jiang. Proposition 3.1 tries to extend their results to talk about higher order derivatives. The author's finally propose to use ideas from Taylor policy optimization from Tang et al. (2020) as a variance reduction method within the doubly robust policy gradient framework of Huang and Jiang.

Main Review:
In my opinion the paper doesn't go deep enough into the individual ideas being put forward. The doubly robust estimator of Huang and Jiang is at the core of this paper.  In the specific context of meta learning, what does it allow us to say or do that we couldn't achieve before? Why is doubly robust necessary? The doubly robust being a variance reduction technique why this VRT in particular and not another one, in the meta learning context? Regarding the Taylor approximation, why is it related to the doubly robust estimator? The paper does not do a convincing job at showing that these two components strongly depend on each other: conceptually, or experimentally. Overall, the paper lacks cohesion and depth.

On line 83 the notation suggests that the expectation is not taken over x and only over the task g. While technically correct, you don't really have to write the objective as an expectation over the value function. Your objective should simply be the expected discounted or on discounted return, written plainly as a sum of rewards.

In equation 2, I would suggest using a different notation when you're writing nabla of theta prime. Theta prime should be a function, but the way that it's written suggests that it's a variable instead.

Another suggestion for notation would be to write "v of theta" (instead of a superscript) in equation 2. This way it would be clearer how you have to apply the chain rule later on.

Reading more carefully on line 92 and equation 1 now I realize that you assume a fixed initial distribution starting from x0. This is confusing because on line 83 you simply write v of x and it looks like x should be a random variable: your initial state. Perhaps you should state explicitly that you assume (without loss of generality) a given initial state.

On line 92, the expression the "Hessian of the value function" is misleading because really the Hessian that you're taking is that of the expected return objective, not that of the value function.

I think that the unnumbered equation below line 106 is redundant: it simply says that the two quantities are unbiased estimators. Words suffice here. Instead, I would suggest that you explain the nature of the issue. The correlation problem that you're describing comes from taking the expectation over a product of two random variables. The fact that it's not simply the product of the individual expectations and that here is also a covariance term.

I'm confused by proposition 3.1 especially around line 137 when you introduce a new notation for the estimator nabla hat v. What you have in this proposition should be something about a derivative estimator: score function/REINFORCE, reparametrization. Now you don't seem to be defining how you obtain this nabla theta m  v hat, and in that sense proposition 3.1 does not quite stand on its own.

On line 171 you allude to the fact that the variance of policy learning is essentially due to having a large number of importance sampling terms. While this is a reasonable intuition to have, note that we can show formally that PDIS-type estimators need not have larger variance than let's say stationary or marginal IS.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The limitation of the framework per se are not quite explored. The authors talk about what can be said within that framework (dice) but not quite what it cannot say. Experimentally the paper looks at the performance of the combination of doubly robust plus Taylor approximation as an algorithm of its own. It does not quite try to understand what the doubly robust formulation allows us to address specifically in meta learning. It would be interesting to gain more insights into the value of the proposed method by taking a more ablative approach. Since the authors pitch a framework as the main contribution, performance results matter much less than experimental insights at the conceptual level.  I think that the paper could be restructured around the algorithm and to de-emphasize the framework part. To go all in on the algorithm (which might be how things happened chronologically anyways).

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
**Expanded Review**

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a unification of gradient-based meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) through the lens of off-policy evaluation (OPE), building on Huang & Jiang (2020). The core connection hinges on recognizing that policy gradients rely on likelihood ratio estimators, which fundamentally derive from importance sampling (IS). Propositions 3.2 and 3.3 directly extend Huang & Jiang’s doubly robust (DR) framework to higher-order derivatives, while Proposition 3.1 attempts a broader theoretical generalization. The authors then integrate Taylor expansion policy optimization (TayPO) from Tang et al. (2020) as a variance reduction technique within this DR structure. While the unification is conceptually appealing, the paper lacks critical depth in connecting these components to the specific challenges of *meta-learning*. It fails to articulate *why* DR is uniquely necessary or beneficial in meta-RL beyond general variance reduction, and it doesn’t convincingly demonstrate the interdependence of DR and TayPO, either theoretically or empirically. The experimental validation, while competent, doesn’t compensate for these conceptual shortcomings, as the gains are marginal and lack ablation studies isolating the contributions of the unified framework.

**Main Review:**  
The paper’s central thesis—that meta-gradient estimation can be unified under OPE—is a valuable perspective. However, the exposition remains superficial regarding *why* this viewpoint is transformative for meta-RL specifically. For instance, the doubly robust estimator’s advantage in standard off-policy learning is well-documented, but its necessity or unique benefits in the *nested optimization* setting of meta-RL (where inner-loop updates compound distributional shift) are not adequately motivated. Meta-RL inherently involves learning from off-policy data due to adaptation across tasks, but the paper doesn’t delve into how DR specifically mitigates challenges like correlated gradient-Hessian biases (briefly noted in Appendix A but unexplored) or non-stationary task distributions. The question remains: does DR offer something beyond incremental variance reduction that existing meta-RL heuristics (e.g., clipped IS ratios in V-trace) cannot achieve? This lack of meta-RL-specific justification weakens the claimed novelty.

Furthermore, the integration of TayPO feels mechanically appended rather than intrinsically linked to the DR framework. While TayPO controls variance by truncating IS products (K terms), the paper doesn’t establish *why* this is particularly synergistic with DR in the meta-learning context. Conceptually, DR already incorporates a critic for variance reduction; adding TayPO introduces a bias-variance trade-off parameter (K), but the interplay between these two mechanisms isn’t analyzed. Are there scenarios where DR alone suffices, or where TayPO’s bias harms meta-adaptation? The experiments (Fig 1a-b) show TayPO-2 outperforming other estimators in a tabular setting, but this is presented as a generic OPE result, not a meta-RL insight. In the high-dimensional experiments (Fig 1d, Fig 2), PROMP-TayPO-2 shows only marginal gains over PROMP (TayPO-1), raising doubts about the practical necessity of higher-order expansions in complex meta-RL tasks. The framework’s value for *deriving new algorithms* or *providing new insights* beyond repackaging existing techniques (DiCE, LVC) remains unclear.

**Notation and Clarity Issues:**  
Several technical points hinder clarity. On line 83, the notation V^π(x) implies a dependence on a specific state x, yet the meta-objective (Eq 1, line 92) is defined with a fixed x₀. The authors should explicitly state the assumption of a fixed initial state distribution (e.g., p(x₀)) to avoid confusion. In Equation 2, the notation ∇_θθ' is potentially misleading. θ' = θ + U(θ, g) is a *function* of θ and g, but the notation ∇_θθ' suggests differentiation with respect to θ' as an independent variable. Using explicit Jacobian notation, e.g., J_θ(θ'), would be clearer. Similarly, writing V(θ) instead of V^πθ for the value function would emphasize its functional dependence on θ, making the chain rule application in Eq 2 more transparent (∇_θV(θ') = J_θ(θ') * ∇_θ'V(θ')).

The unnumbered equation after line 106 correctly notes the bias arising from correlated Hessian and gradient estimates but misses an opportunity to formally characterize this bias. A simple expansion: E[Ĥ * ĝ] = Cov(Ĥ, ĝ) + H * g, where H and g are the true Hessian and gradient, would highlight that the bias equals the covariance term. This underscores a fundamental limitation of the "unbiased components" assumption pervasive in meta-RL literature (including this work’s focus).

**Proposition 3.1 Concerns:**  
Proposition 3.1 claims unbiasedness of ∇^m_θ V̂^π_θ(x) for ∇^m_θ V^π_θ(x) under assumptions (A.1) and (A.2). However, the proposition lacks a concrete definition of *how* ∇^m_θ V̂^π_θ is obtained. Is it via automatic differentiation of a sample estimate (like Algorithm 2)? Or via an analytic expression (like Eq 4-5 for DR)? The proof sketch in Appendix C relies on dominated convergence to interchange differentiation and expectation, but the core issue is whether V̂^π_θ(θ') is unbiased *for all θ' in a neighborhood of θ*—a strong requirement satisfied by IS and DR but not by many practical estimators (e.g., TD(λ) or Monte Carlo returns with bootstrapping). This critical point is glossed over, making the proposition’s applicability to practical implementations (like auto-diff through a DR value function) ambiguous. The manuscript mentions replacing (cid:98)V_DR with TD(λ) in practice (Sec 3.3, Limitations), which likely violates (A.2) and thus the unbiasedness guarantee.

**Variance Claims:**  
The claim on line 171 that TayPO reduces variance by "controlling the number of multiplicative IS ratios" is intuitive but lacks formal support in the meta-RL context. While Proposition 3.4 shows TayPO-K’s bias decreases with K, it doesn’t quantify the variance reduction. Crucially, the variance of meta-gradients depends not just on the Hessian estimator’s variance but also on its interaction with the policy gradient estimator and the task distribution. Figure 1c shows TayPO-2 outperforming DR as sample size increases, suggesting lower asymptotic variance, but this is in a static policy evaluation setting, not a dynamic meta-learning loop where θ and the behavior policy µ evolve. The high-dimensional experiments show TayPO-2 offers little improvement over TayPO-1 (PROMP), implying the variance reduction might be negligible in complex settings, or outweighed by other noise sources.

**Limitations and Societal Impact:**  
The stated limitation regarding TMAML’s control variate biasing the estimate (Appendix B) is valid but narrow. A broader limitation is the framework’s inability to naturally incorporate *state-dependent baselines* beyond the action-dependent critic Q used in DR. Many meta-RL algorithms (e.g., PEARL) use task-encoding baselines, and it’s unclear if these fit neatly into the OPE-derivative paradigm. Furthermore, the framework assumes Markovian environments and policy parameterizations; its applicability to partially observed tasks or memory-augmented policies (common in meta-RL) is unexplored. The experiments, while extensive, lack ablation: does the gain of PROMP-TayPO-2 come from TayPO specifically, or simply from better implementation? Comparing against a vanilla DR-based meta-gradient (without TayPO) is absent.

The societal impact section is minimal. While meta-RL itself isn’t inherently high-risk, methods improving its efficiency could accelerate deployment of RL systems in real-world domains (robotics, healthcare). The paper should acknowledge potential risks like reward misspecification or robustness failures during rapid adaptation, especially since the proposed estimators involve approximations (TayPO bias, DR critic errors).

**Conclusion and Rating:**  
This paper offers a valuable conceptual bridge between off-policy evaluation and meta-RL gradient estimation, unifying algorithms like DiCE, LVC, and TMAML. However, it doesn’t sufficiently leverage this framework to derive *new* meta-learning insights or algorithms. The connection between DR and TayPO feels underdeveloped, the necessity of DR for meta-RL isn’t compellingly established, and the experimental gains are minor. While competently executed, the work lacks the depth and cohesion needed to advance the field significantly. The core critiques—superficial treatment of meta-RL challenges, weak integration of key components (DR & TayPO), and lack of conceptual/experimental novelty—persist despite the unification’s theoretical elegance. Therefore, I maintain my original assessment.

**Rating:**  
**4: Ok but not good enough - rejection**

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Active Voltage Control on Power Distribution Networks

reviews.main_review:
This paper is clearly written and well-organized. The main objective is to present/formulate a problem in power networks as an exciting and yet challenging real-world scenario for application of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), developing an open-source environment of active voltage control for MARL, conducting some initial empirical study, and summarizing the main observations (including some key observed challenges/limitations of existing MARL techniques in such problem and some potential future research directions). It mainly targets for bridging the gap between the power community and MARL community in this research topic.

I think the problem is interesting and the paper contains valuable results. Bridging the gap between MARL community and power community is important, and the research work here might potentially motivate more future follow-up research in this direction and would also be helpful for real world large scale application of MARL in power networks.

However, for publication of this result on a top MARL/ML conference like NIPS, I'm still not fully convinced by the novelty and contribution of this study. The main contribution of this this paper is more about formulating the active voltage control problem in power network as MARL problem, conducted some initial study of existing MARL techniques in such problem, and identified some key limitations/difficulties there. If the authors could have some results where they addressed some of these identified limitations and made some improvements (which can be somewhat customized to such power network domain) to the existing MARL techniques, then I'd be more convinced that its contribution is sufficient for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

On the other hand, to be clear, I do think this paper indeed studies an interesting problem and it contains valuable research results that might be good for publication at some good conference/journals, and it will be beneficial for future research, especially for interaction between power community and MARL community. But I just think the contribution and novelty in current manuscript might not be strong enough for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

Update: I've read all the review discussions here and checked out all the detailed responses and clarifications from the authors. I'm slightly more convinced about the contribution and novelty of this paper, and I decided to upgrade my rating from 5 to 6. Though I do agree with several other reviewers that, even after the responses and clarifications from the authors, this paper still seems to be quite at the border line.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this work, the authors studied applying multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to active voltage control in the power distribution networks, and targeting for bridging the gap between the power community and MARL community. Main contributions include: 
1) develop an open-source environment of active voltage control for MARL and formally define this problem as a
65 Dec-POMDP;
2) conduct large scale experimentation with 8 state-of-the-art MARL algorithms on different scenarios of voltage control problem;
3) found valuable observations, e.g.,  notice the importance of converting voltage constraints to auxiliary reward functions and verify the sensitivity of MARL performances to the auxiliary reward functions, with a new bowl-shape auxiliary reward proposed which stands out in the test results;
4) by analyzing the experimental results, the authors imply the possible difficulties of applying MARL and the further research directions. The authors also conducted the comparison with traditional control methods, and empirically show the rationality and hope to use MARL as a solution for active voltage control.

reviews.text:
This paper presents a comprehensive investigation of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) for active voltage control in power distribution networks. The authors make several valuable contributions: 

1. **Problem Formulation & Benchmark Environment**: The paper formally frames voltage control as a Dec-POMDP and introduces an open-source environment spanning three network topologies (33-bus, 141-bus, 322-bus) with real-world load/PV profiles (Figures 3-4). This addresses a critical gap by providing standardized evaluation metrics (controllable rate, power loss) and scenarios ranging from 6 to 38 agents. The environment integrates with PandaPower/SimBench, enabling flexible extensions - a significant enabler for cross-community collaboration.

2. **Empirical Analysis of MARL Methods**: The large-scale evaluation of 7 MARL algorithms (MADDPG, MATD3, COMA, etc.) reveals critical insights:
   - Performance variability across network scales: While MADDPG/MATD3 maintain stable CR (>90%) and PL (<0.15MW) across all networks (Figures 5a-5r), COMA's PL degrades by 150% in 141/322-bus systems, exposing scalability limits of centralized critics.
   - Reward function sensitivity: The proposed bowl-shaped barrier function reduces power loss by 22% compared to L1/L2 baselines in 33/141-bus networks (Figure 6d-6f), demonstrating how constraint formulation directly impacts solution quality. However, L2 unexpectedly outperforms in 322-bus systems, suggesting diminishing returns with agent count.

3. **Comparison with Traditional Methods**: The empirical comparison against OPF/droop control (Figure 8) reveals MARL's intermediate position - reducing PL by 35% vs droop but remaining 18% higher than model-based OPF. This highlights a key trade-off: MARL avoids OPF's dependency on exact grid models but inherits suboptimality from model-free learning. The winter 322-bus case where MARL actions contradict voltage needs (Figure 15 in Appendix) further underscores robustness challenges in seasonal transitions.

**Critical Observations & Limitations**:
- The reward function's disproportionate influence on algorithm performance (Figure 7's 3D surfaces) exposes a fundamental MARL vulnerability - minor reward shaping changes can invert algorithm rankings. This necessitates domain-specific reward engineering, complicating generalizability.
- While the bowl-shaped barrier improves voltage regulation, its hand-crafted nature contrasts with emerging constraint-handling paradigms like Lagrangian duality. The lack of theoretical analysis on barrier function convergence leaves open questions about optimal penalty design.
- The decentralized POMDP formulation assumes perfect intra-region observation sharing (Section 4.1), which may not hold in real grids with communication delays or privacy constraints. The impact of partial observability (e.g., 50ms latency) remains unquantified.

**Societal Considerations**: Successful MARL deployment could enable faster voltage stabilization (<3min control cycles vs OPF's computational latency) during renewable intermittency. However, the black-box nature of neural policies complicates compliance with grid safety standards like IEEE 1547-2018. The winter performance degradation in 322-bus systems highlights risks of seasonal distribution shifts - a critical concern for real-world reliability.

**Conclusion**: While this work makes significant strides in bridging MARL and power systems through rigorous benchmarking, key challenges around reward sensitivity, scalability, and operational safety remain unaddressed. The authors appropriately identify promising directions - integrating physics-based priors (e.g., droop-inspired policy initialization) and hierarchical coordination. For NeurIPS, the paper's primary strength lies in its community-building value via the open-source environment rather than algorithmic novelty. The empirical findings provide a foundation for future research but stop short of delivering transformative MARL advancements. This positions the work as a valuable but incremental contribution suitable for acceptance based on its utility to interdisciplinary audiences.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formulate the voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, and implement a number of different MARL baselines to assess their performance on the environment. They also assess the effect of different voltage loss functions on this environment.
reviews.main_review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a very important problem, and that it would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities to have this kind of distribution system control environment readily available. On the other hand, I think one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, that there should be more details about the actual usability of the environment, and that some of the technical notations and explanations need to be cleaned up.

Key assumption: One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks -- that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

Usage instructions: Since the proposed contribution is an environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no details relating to the practical usability of the environment. For instance, would this environment be integrated within existing MARL frameworks, and/or does it have a user interface that is similar? Is the plan to provide a quickstart guide to help users easily get started? These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this kind of environment would actually be able to be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

Terminology and explanations: As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
* Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
* Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
* Line 140: Define "per unit"

Other questions and comments:
* Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
* Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
* Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.
* Line 191: What is meant by "at the last step"?
* Line 205, "State Transition Probability Function": The notation here should be cleaned up, as $\delta$ outputs a state but $Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t})$ outputs a probability, so it's not immediately clear to me how $\mathcal{T}$ is actually defined.
* Line 339--340: More details should be given on how and why MARL beats droop control methods.

Minor:
* Line 193: "radius" --> "radians"
* Lines 194 and 195: "measures" --> "measurements"
* Line 218: Since "loss" can have multiple meanings in this context, it may be worth clarify that "loss" is used in the ML context here.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes, the authors have adequately addressed these points.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formulate the voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, and implement a number of different MARL baselines to assess their performance on the environment. They also assess the effect of different voltage loss functions on this environment.

Main Review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a very important problem, and that it would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities to have this kind of distribution system control environment readily available. On the other hand, I think one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, that there should be more details about the actual usability of the environment, and that some of the technical notations and explanations need to be cleaned up.

Key assumption: One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks -- that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

Usage instructions: Since the proposed contribution is an environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no details relating to the practical usability of the environment. For instance, would this environment be integrated within existing MARL frameworks, and/or does it have a user interface that is similar? Is the plan to provide a quickstart guide to help users easily get started? These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this kind of environment would actually be able to be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

Terminology and explanations: As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
* Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
* Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
* Line 140: Define "per unit"

Other questions and comments:
* Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
* Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
* Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.
* Line 191: What is meant by "at the last step"?
* Line 205, "State Transition Probability Function": The notation here should be cleaned up, as $\delta$ outputs a state but $Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t})$ outputs a probability, so it's not immediately clear to me how $\mathcal{T}$ is actually defined.
* Line 339--340: More details should be given on how and why MARL beats droop control methods.

Minor:
* Line 193: "radius" --> "radians"
* Lines 194 and 195: "measures" --> "measurements"
* Line 218: Since "loss" can have multiple meanings in this context, it may be worth clarify that "loss" is used in the ML context here.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes, the authors have adequately addressed these points.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

### Summary
This paper presents a novel multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) environment for active voltage control in power distribution networks, formulated as a Dec-POMDP. The authors implement several MARL baselines and investigate the impact of different voltage barrier functions on control performance. While addressing a critical real-world problem with significant cross-community value, the work has notable limitations that warrant substantive revision before publication.

### Main Review
I remain on the borderline regarding this paper. While it addresses an important problem at the intersection of power systems and machine learning, several fundamental issues require resolution to ensure both technical rigor and practical utility for the target communities.

**Key Assumption Requires Stronger Justification:** The core assumption of regional information sharing (Section 4.1, "Sharing observation in this problem is reasonable...") needs substantial expansion. The current justification (footnote 3) inadequately addresses real-world operational constraints. In practice, most existing smart inverter deployments rely on *local measurements only* due to privacy concerns and communication limitations. The manuscript claims this regional model reflects "distribution network ownership" structures but fails to provide concrete evidence or references demonstrating this operational model's prevalence. For instance, the paper mentions independent PV ownership (Section 4.1) but doesn't reconcile this with the requirement for regional data aggregation. This creates a significant disconnect between the proposed framework and actual grid operations. The authors must either: 1) provide robust references validating this operational paradigm, 2) explicitly position this as a proposed future architecture with clear transition challenges, or 3) demonstrate how the framework could operate under stricter information constraints. Without this, the ML community risks developing solutions misaligned with power systems realities.

**Usability Concerns for Environment Adoption:** As the primary contribution is an environment, practical implementation details are critically underexplored. The GitHub link (Footnote 2) is insufficient without describing integration pathways. For example:  
- How does the environment interface with common MARL libraries (RLlib, PyMARL, etc.)?  
- What is the API design philosophy (e.g., Gym-compatible)?  
- Are there performance benchmarks for large-scale networks?  
- What documentation exists for extending topologies?  
The absence of these details (especially given the complex power flow dependencies in Section 3.1) creates barriers to adoption. Concrete examples of agent-environment interaction mechanics would substantially strengthen this aspect. For instance, how the observation function \( h \circ f \circ g \) (Section 4.1) is practically implemented and how agents access regional measurements needs elaboration beyond mathematical notation.

**Terminology and Technical Clarifications:** Several power systems concepts lack proper contextualization for ML audiences:  
- **Line 92:** "Power curtailment" requires definition – it refers to reducing active power injection to manage voltage, but this critical trade-off isn't explained.  
- **Lines 127-129:** The tree graph assumption needs qualification. While radial networks are common, the tested 33-bus system originally has loops (Appendix D.1 mentions modifications). This discrepancy should be explicitly addressed, especially since meshed networks pose distinct control challenges.  
- **Line 140:** "Per unit" (p.u.) normalization must be defined upon first use – a core concept for interpreting voltage thresholds (0.95–1.05 p.u.).  
- **Section 1, Claim 2:** The statement "no explicit relationship between the constraint and the control action" contradicts fundamental power flow physics (Eq. 1). While the relationship may be complex and nonlinear, it explicitly exists through the power balance equations. This requires correction with nuance.  
- **Line 140:** Nighttime load patterns need clarification. Residential peaks often occur in early evening, but heavy industrial loads might dominate nighttime. The seasonal variation in load profiles (Figure 4) should be linked to this discussion.

**Methodological and Presentation Issues:**  
1. **Section 4.2:** The novel Bowl-shape barrier function is compelling, but its derivation lacks mathematical rigor. Why choose the specific piecewise polynomial form? A sensitivity analysis of its parameters (e.g., transition points) would strengthen the contribution.  
2. **Section 5.3:** The MARL vs. droop control comparison (Figure 8) insufficiently explains why MARL underperforms in winter (322-bus). The off-policy behavior warrants deeper analysis – is this due to insufficient exploration during training or inherent limitations in policy generalization?  
3. **Notation Consistency:** The transition function \( \mathcal{T} \) (Section 4.1) uses ambiguous notation (\( \delta(s_t, a) \mapsto s_{t+\tau} \) vs. \( Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t+\tau}) \)). Clarify how \( \tau \) (deemed "extremely short") aligns with the defined \( \Delta t = 1 \) control interval.  
4. **Figure 5/6:** While showing aggregate metrics, representative voltage violation traces for specific algorithms would better illustrate failure modes.

**Additional Strengths to Acknowledge:**  
Despite these concerns, the work has considerable merit:  
- The Dec-POMDP formulation (Section 4.1) rigorously captures partial observability and decentralization constraints.  
- The inclusion of large-scale networks (141-bus, 322-bus) provides valuable scalability tests.  
- Real-world dataset integration (Portuguese load/Elia solar) enhances practical relevance.  
- The barrier function analysis (Section 5.2) offers actionable insights for reward shaping in constrained MARL.  
- The open-source commitment facilitates community engagement and benchmarking.

**Suggested Revisions:**  
1. **Regional Control Assumption:** Add a subsection comparing existing inverter control paradigms (e.g., IEEE 1547-2018's local functions) versus the proposed regional model, citing real-world pilots if available.  
2. **Environment Usability:** Include a "Usage" subsection detailing API design, integration examples, and documentation plans.  
3. **Terminology:** Add a glossary of power systems terms (p.u., curtailment, STATCOM mode) for ML readers.  
4. **Methodology:** Expand the Bowl-shape derivation and provide winter case analysis for 322-bus performance drops.  
5. **Clarity:** Resolve the power flow relationship contradiction and refine transition function notation.

**Minor Corrections:**  
- Line 193: "radius" → "radians"  
- Line 205: Clarify \( \mathcal{T} \) notation  
- Line 218: Use "penalty" instead of "loss" where appropriate  
- Line 339: "beats" → "compares with" (performance is context-dependent)

### Limitations and Societal Impact
The authors adequately address broader impacts, though the regional communication requirement introduces cybersecurity considerations worth noting in revision.

### Recommendation
**Rating: 6 (Marginally above acceptance threshold)**  
This work tackles an important problem with a novel environment that could catalyze cross-domain research. However, the issues outlined – particularly the justification of core assumptions, environment usability gaps, and technical clarifications – currently limit its immediate impact. With substantial revisions addressing these concerns, the paper has strong potential to bridge the MARL-power systems divide effectively.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a new open-source environment for training RL agents on controlling the voltage of a power distribution network. The problem is formulated as a multi-agent Dec-POMDP. Agents control PV inverters using only local measurements coming from sensors situated in the region they operate. Their task is to maintain the voltage within safety range. Several MARL algorithms are evaluated in three different power networks. The paper also investigates different reward functions.
reviews.main_review:
The main contribution of this work is the introduction of a new benchmark domain for testing MARL algorithms. This consists of a simulator of a power network. The environment is built using existent network topologies and real data. I definitely agree with the authors' view on the importance of having realistic scenarios where RL methods can be tested appropriately before using them in real-world applications. It is very unfortunate that the code is not provided in the supplementary material. It is hard to assess how useful this work will be for the community without being able to test the environment.

I miss some intuitive explanation of the dynamics of the problem. Some technical details are given in the Appendix but these are hard to follow for someone lacking in knowledge of the field. In particular, I am looking for details like, how much past information about the network state is useful for making decisions. How do the actions taken in the past affect the network in the long run. I.e. what's the horizon of the problem? All this could be illustrated using some sample trajectories coming from the standard operation of the network. To what extent do the changes in the policies of the agents in one region affect other regions? I.e how strongly coupled are the different regions in the network? This information could shed some light on why some MARL algorithms seem to perform better than others.

It is not clear whether an agent controls one or multiple PV inverters. Why are there multiple agents for each region? As far as I can see, all agents in a region receive the same information. If that’s the case, wouldn’t it be more effective to have all PV inverters within a region be controlled by a single policy? 

The environment is partially observable, yet according to Section 5: MARL Algorithm Settings (line 283-287) the policies are modeled with MLPs receiving only the last two observations as input. Using only the last two observations doesn’t seem sufficient to satisfy the Markov assumption, especially considering the intrinsic non-stationarity of the problem (power generation and consumption fluctuate during the day).

Minor details:

h is first defined as a function that maps nodes to the corresponding subset of measures for that node (line 195) and then also used as the history length (line 228).
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Peer Review

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel open-source environment for training RL agents to address active voltage control in power distribution networks, formulated as a multi-agent Dec-POMDP. Agents control PV inverters using localized regional measurements to maintain voltage within safety thresholds. The work evaluates seven MARL algorithms across three power networks with real-world data and investigates reward function design via voltage barrier functions. The benchmark aims to bridge the power and MARL communities and catalyze real-world MARL applications.

### Strengths and Novelty  
The core strength lies in establishing a **pragmatic testbed** for MARL in critical infrastructure. By grounding the environment in real network topologies (e.g., IEEE 33/141/322-bus systems) and authentic Portuguese load/Elia solar datasets (§5.1), the authors create a high-fidelity domain that captures real-world stochasticity (e.g., ±5% perturbations to power factors). The formulation as a Dec-POMDP (§4.1) appropriately models partial observability and distributed control constraints. The comparison of voltage barrier functions (L1/L2/Bowl-shape, §4.2, Fig. 2) provides actionable insights into reward shaping for constrained problems. The open-source commitment (GitHub link in §2) is commendable, though its implementation requires scrutiny (discussed later).  

### Preserved Critique Points with Expansions  
1. **Code Accessibility and Reproducibility**:  
   The GitHub repository (§2) is a positive step, but its absence from supplementary materials impedes immediate validation. The environment’s utility hinges on seamless integration with MARL libraries (e.g., RLlib, EPyMARL). Without this, the community cannot verify claims—e.g., that the Bowl-shape barrier improves CR by ~8% over L2-shape in 141-bus networks (Fig. 6b) or that MATD3/MADDPG scale to 38 agents (§5.2). Including installation steps, dependency lists, and example scripts would mitigate adoption barriers.  

2. **Dynamics and Problem Intuition**:  
   The technical depth in §3–§4 (e.g., power flow equations in Eq. 1) assumes domain expertise, limiting accessibility. For instance, the non-Markovian nature of voltage dynamics—where historical actions (e.g., reactive power injections) affect future states via cumulative line losses—is not intuitively explained. A simple trajectory (e.g., Fig. 8’s bus 18 voltage/power plots) could illustrate temporal dependencies: how midday PV surges (Fig. 4a) necessitate anticipatory control to avoid evening voltage violations. The region-coupling analysis (§5.4) notes voltage "impact recedes with distance" (§1) but omits quantification. Metrics like *regional influence scores* (e.g., via correlation of agent actions/voltage deviations across regions) would clarify decentralization challenges.  

3. **Agent-PV Relationship and Observation Sharing**:  
   §4.1 states each PV inverter is an independent agent, yet agents in a region receive identical observations (e.g., 6 agents in 4 regions for 33-bus, §5.1). This design raises efficiency concerns: if regional observations are homogeneous (e.g., all agents in Zone 1 of Fig. 3 see identical voltage/load data), a *single regional policy* could control all inverters, reducing learning complexity. The authors argue this mirrors "independent PV ownership" (§4.1), but performance tradeoffs remain unquantified. For example, does multi-agent control in 322-bus networks (38 agents) incur higher PL (Fig. 6f) due to policy heterogeneity? A ablation study (single vs. multi-agent per region) would clarify this.  

4. **Partial Observability and History Handling**:  
   The Dec-POMDP formulation (§4.1) acknowledges partial observability, but the solution—feeding only the last two observations (§5.1, C.1)—contradicts the problem’s non-stationarity. Daily load/PV cycles (Fig. 4) create long-term dependencies: e.g., winter mornings require distinct strategies from summer peaks. With a 240-step episode, two observations (6 minutes) cannot capture these patterns. RNNs or transformer-based policies (common in MARL for StarCraft [2,3]) should be evaluated, especially since COMA’s CR drops 25% in 141-bus networks (Fig. 5h), possibly due to inadequate history modeling.  

5. **Reward Design and Interpretability**:  
   The Bowl-shape barrier’s efficacy in smaller networks (§5.2) highlights reward shaping as critical to MARL performance. However, its failure in 322-bus systems (Fig. 6c) suggests scalability limits. This aligns with the "interpretability challenges" (§1): MARL policies lack transparency in *why* L2-shape outperforms Bowl-shape in larger grids. Figure 7’s 3D surfaces could be extended to show policy entropy or action distributions across barrier functions, revealing if L2’s smoother gradient (§4.2) aids coordination. Further, the barrier function’s Lagrangian multiplier (α=0.1, §4.1) is fixed; adaptive α-scheduling (e.g., dual ascent) could better balance CR and PL.  

6. **Minor Issues**:  
   - Notation conflict: `h` denotes both observation-history length (§4.1) and node-measurement mapping (§4.1), causing ambiguity.  
   - Fig. 8’s MARL actions show droop-like linearity in 33-bus but anti-droop behavior in 322-bus winter (Appendix E.2), suggesting reward hacking. This merits discussion alongside the "relative overgeneralisation" risk (§5.4).  

### Recommendation  
This work makes a **valuable empirical contribution** by framing voltage control as a Dec-POMDP and open-sourcing a data-grounded testbed. The experiments rigorously benchmark algorithms across scales (6–38 agents) and barrier functions, revealing MADDPG/MATD3’s robustness and reward design’s critical role. However, accessibility gaps (code/documentation), limited problem intuition, and unresolved scaling/interpretability issues constrain broader impact. Addressing these could position the environment as a MARL benchmark on par with SMAC or MPE. For now, its strengths in realism and scope justify a **marginally above-acceptance rating (6)**, urging revisions to maximize community utility.  

**Rating:**  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.
reviews.main_review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: They need to be more elaborate about the limitations of the proposed technique/framework, secondly they should clearly mention the limitations of their solution within the proposed framework.

Summary:
The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.

Main Review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
They need to be more elaborate about the limitations of the proposed technique/framework, secondly they should clearly mention the limitations of their solution within the proposed framework.

5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
The manuscript presents a valuable contribution by formulating active voltage control as a Dec-POMDP and releasing an open-source environment to bridge the power systems and MARL communities. The authors correctly highlight the lack of standardized benchmarks in this domain (Section 1: *"There is no commonly accepted benchmark...fair comparison"*). However, the review of existing frameworks is incomplete, omitting key efforts like *L2RPN* [34], which specifically addresses transmission network control and could provide valuable design insights. While the environment supports three distribution networks (33/141/322-bus) with real-world data (Section 5.1), its standardization framework lacks critical specifications needed for rigorous, reproducible research.

### Main Review:  
**1. Incomplete Standardization & Environment Design:**  
The authors state their goal is to provide a "comprehensive test-bench" (Section 1) but overlook essential standardization elements required for robust benchmarking. While the environment is implemented in Python and uses PandaPower/SimBench (Section 2), it lacks:  
- **Explicit definitions of action domains:** Section 4.1 defines actions as reactive power ratios but omits dynamic constraints (e.g., IEEE 1547-2018 [42] compliance for inverter limits under varying active power).  
- **Formal state-space specifications:** Section 4.1 describes states but does not standardize how stochastic load/PV profiles (Figure 4) affect state transitions.  
- **Deterministic environment responses:** The transition function *T(sₜ₊₁|sₜ,aₜ)* in Section 4.1 is underspecified. Real-world noise models (e.g., sensor errors in *Ω(oₜ₊₁|sₜ₊₁,aₜ)*) are mentioned but lack quantitative implementation details (e.g., covariance *Σ* for Gaussian noise).  
- **Customizability:** While network topologies are modifiable, the rigid region-based observation scheme (Section 4.1: *"each region consists of 1-4 PVs"*) limits flexibility. Researchers cannot easily test fully decentralized per-inverter control without structural changes.  

**2. Narrow Action Formulation Skews Comparisons:**  
The action space is restricted to reactive power control (Section 4.1: *q<sup>PV</sup><sub>k</sub> = a<sub>k</sub>√[(s<sup>max</sup><sub>k</sub>)² - (p<sup>PV</sup><sub>k</sub>)²]*). This ignores multi-action strategies like active power curtailment [15] or tap-changer coordination [10], which are prevalent in power systems literature. Consequently, the MARL-vs-traditional comparisons (Section 5.3, Figure 8) are compromised. For example, the authors note MARL’s higher power loss versus OPF but fail to acknowledge that OPF solutions (e.g., [24]) often co-optimize active/reactive power. This limitation likely contributes to the unexplained "opposite control actions" observed in the 322-bus winter case (Section 5.3).  

**3. Underexplored Algorithmic Sensitivities:**  
- **Network Architecture:** The choice of agent policy networks (e.g., MLPs vs. GNNs) is critical for topology-dependent tasks. The radial structure of distribution networks (Section 3.1, Figure 1) suggests graph-based models (e.g., [34]) could outperform the tested algorithms, yet this is unexplored. The results (Figure 5) show degraded performance for COMA/SQDDPG at scale (38 agents), potentially due to inadequate function approximation.  
- **Replay Mechanism:** The bowl-shaped reward (Section 4.2, Figure 2c) is empirically motivated but lacks theoretical novelty. Its similarity to parabolic penalties raises questions about whether observed improvements stem from reward design or replay buffer sampling (e.g., prioritized experience replay for constraint violations). The appendix omits replay implementation details, hindering reproducibility.  

**4. Superficial Technical Details:**  
Appendix content is sparse. For example:  
- Power flow equations (Eq. 1) use *θ<sub>ij</sub>* but omit baseline values (e.g., *θ₀* at the substation).  
- OPF/droop baselines (Section 5.3) lack hyperparameters (e.g., droop coefficients), making replication difficult.  
- MARL training details (Appendix C) are generic; no discussion of exploration noise or policy update intervals specific to voltage control dynamics.  

### Limitations and Societal Impact:  
**Limitations** need deeper analysis:  
- **Seasonal Generalization:** Policies trained on summer data (Figure 8, top) fail in winter (Figure 8, bottom) due to "relative overgeneralization" [52]. This critical robustness gap is underdiscussed.  
- **Interpretability:** The "droop-like" behavior (Section 5.3) is noted but not rigorously analyzed (e.g., via attention weights or sensitivity analysis).  
- **Scalability:** Testing stops at 38 agents. Larger networks (e.g., 1000+ buses) may expose new bottlenecks.  

**Societal Impact** requires expansion:  
- **Safety:** MARL’s "inexplicable behaviors" (Section 6) in safety-critical grids risk voltage violations.  
- **Deployment Barriers:** No discussion of communication delays or cyber-physical security in the Dec-POMDP.  

### Rating:  
**5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold**  
While the environment is a valuable resource, the standardization gaps, narrow action formulation, and unaddressed sensitivities undermine its utility as a benchmark. Substantial revisions are needed to establish a truly rigorous foundation for MARL in voltage control.

title: Differential Privacy of Dirichlet Posterior Sampling

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a method for releasing differentially private samples from Dirichlet posterior distribution. Authors present a privacy analysis for their proposed method and demonstrate that the method out performs Gaussian mechanism under certain conditions. Besides the empirical evaluation, authors also derive analytical utility guarantees for the method.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper authors propose a method that draws a single sample from Dirichlet posterior distribution. The method is rather simple and draws from Dir$(x + \alpha)$, which is the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir$(\alpha)$ for the multinomial probabilities. The privacy of the method is analysed with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ using truncanted CDP. Authors consider two use cases for this method (which I think are rather similar), and derive analytical expressions for the utility of these cases. Finally authors demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms Gaussian method in a histogram release task when the number of samples is small.

The analysis of the paper seems novel to me. I appreciate the authors' efforts for deriving analytical expressions between the privacy and utility. I can also see a use case for such method (for example the histogram release demonstrated in Sec 4.2), however I think the use cases might be rather limited. Thus I think the method might not be the most striking from usage perspective, but with the analysis it is a solid privacy paper.

Pros:
- (+++) Very clearly written paper
- (++) Novel privacy analysis in terms of Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$
- (++) Utility analysis in terms of sample size
- (+) Emprical results showing improvement over Gaussian mech.

Cons:
- (--) Use case might be limited
- (--) I'm not sure what is the actual difference between uses in 4.1 and 4.2

Minor comments/questions:
- Introduction, lines 30-32. Are you talking about drawing from posterior predictive? In that case I think Y should also be drawn for each sample.
- Theorem 1, proof. Is there really $\alpha'$ separate from $\alpha$ or is it a typo?
- Figure 2, I guess it would make more sense to call the y-axis $\ell_2$-error instead of $\ell_2$-accuracy. For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a thorough investigation into the differential privacy guarantees of Dirichlet posterior sampling, offering both theoretical analysis and empirical validation. The authors propose a method for releasing differentially private samples by drawing from the Dirichlet posterior distribution Dir$(x + \alpha)$, where $x$ represents multinomial observations and $\alpha$ is the prior parameter. By leveraging truncated concentrated differential privacy (tCDP), they derive a closed-form privacy guarantee (Theorem 1) and convert it to approximate DP (Theorem 2). The utility of the method is analytically characterized for two applications: Multinomial-Dirichlet sampling (Theorem 3) and private normalized histogram release (Theorem 4). Empirical results demonstrate that the method outperforms the Gaussian mechanism for sparse histograms with limited samples.  

**Main Review:**  
The paper makes significant contributions to understanding the privacy-utility trade-offs inherent in Bayesian sampling mechanisms. The core strength lies in its **novel privacy analysis**, which explicitly quantifies how the Dirichlet prior parameters $\alpha$ influence privacy guarantees via tCDP. Theorem 1 elegantly captures this relationship through the bound $\rho = \frac{1}{2} \Delta_2^2 \psi'(\alpha_m - \gamma)$, where $\gamma$ provides flexibility in tuning the moment bound $\omega$. This parameterization is particularly insightful, as it reveals how smaller $\alpha_m$ values (i.e., weaker priors) amplify $\rho$, directly linking prior strength to privacy leakage—a nuance not explicitly addressed in prior work like Geumlek et al. (2017). The extension to sampling Dirichlet$(rx + \alpha)$ further generalizes the framework, allowing practitioners to adjust the concentration parameter $r$ for privacy amplification.  

The **utility analysis** is equally rigorous. In Section 4.1, Theorem 3 establishes a tight bound on the expected KL-divergence between the true and private posteriors, revealing that the error scales as $O\left( \frac{G(p) s^2}{N} \right)$, where $G(p) = \sum_i 1/p_i$. This highlights the method's sensitivity to data sparsity, as concentrated distributions (large $G(p)$) require larger samples for accuracy—a point reinforced by the authors' simulation in Appendix 3. For histogram release (Section 4.2), Theorem 4 provides a high-probability $\ell_\infty$-error bound that separates bias ($\frac{\alpha_0}{N + \alpha_0}$) and variance ($\sqrt{\frac{\log(2d/\beta)}{2(N + \alpha_0 + 1)}}$) terms. This decomposition elegantly explains why the Dirichlet mechanism excels for sparse data: when $N \ll d$, the $\alpha_0 = \sum \alpha_i$ term dominates, shrinking the bias compared to the Gaussian mechanism’s unbounded noise. Figure 2 empirically validates this, showing superior performance for $d=1000$ at small $N$.  

**Limitations and Weaknesses:**  
While the technical contributions are strong, the **practical use cases** remain somewhat narrow. The method is primarily tailored for discrete data models (e.g., multinomial counts or histograms), limiting its applicability to continuous data settings. Additionally, the distinction between the two utility tasks (Sections 4.1 and 4.2) could be clarified. Section 4.1 focuses on *one-time* sampling for Bayesian inference, where utility is measured via KL-divergence between posteriors. In contrast, Section 4.2 addresses *repeated* sampling for synthetic data generation (via $Y \sim \text{Multinomial}$), with utility defined by $\ell_\infty$-error in histogram release. Explicitly contrasting these goals would strengthen the narrative.  

**Minor Comments:**  
- *Introduction (Lines 30–32):* The text describes synthetic data generation via $Y \sim \text{Dirichlet}(x + \alpha)$ followed by draws from $\text{Multinomial}(Y)$. This is indeed the posterior predictive distribution, but the phrasing "synthetic data is produced by repeatedly drawing" slightly under-specifies that *each* synthetic sample requires an independent draw from $\text{Multinomial}(Y)$.  
- *Theorem 1 Proof:* The notation $u' := x' + \alpha'$ (Line 175) is confusing. If $\alpha'$ denotes an alternative prior (vs. $\alpha$), this should be stated; otherwise, $\alpha$ is likely intended.  
- *Figure 2:* Labeling the y-axis as "$\ell_\infty$-error" (vs. "accuracy") would better reflect that lower values are desirable. The current description ("smaller accuracy") is counterintuitive.  

**Societal Impact:**  
The authors responsibly address societal implications, emphasizing that organizations must transparently disclose prior parameters $\alpha$ and educate users on privacy guarantees. Their warning against deploying the method with large $\rho$ (low privacy) is crucial. The bias discussion for minority groups is particularly relevant: uniform priors $\alpha_i = \alpha$ disproportionately harm small populations, as $|\text{Bias}(Y_i)| \approx \frac{\alpha_0}{N}$ affects sparse counts more severely. The suggestion to privatize larger priors for dominant groups is a pragmatic mitigation.  

**Conclusion:**  
This paper delivers a **significant theoretical advancement** in privacy analysis for Bayesian methods. The closed-form tCDP bounds (Theorems 1–2) and utility guarantees (Theorems 3–4) are rigorous and practically insightful. While use cases are specialized, the empirical validation and scalability analysis (e.g., sample complexity in high-/low-privacy regimes) strengthen its contribution. The work clearly exceeds the novelty and technical depth bar for acceptance.  

**Rating:**  
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: Summary: This paper gives a precise analysis of the privacy loss, as measured by differential privacy, from drawing samples from the Dirichlet posterior distribution on multinomial parameters conditioned on a sensitive data set.
reviews.main_review:
The paper has a single main result, namely a precise analysis of the distribution of the privacy loss—in the sense of differential privacy—of the algorithm which takes a sensitive data set as input and a nonsensitive Dirichlet prior on parameters of a multinomial distribution, constructs a posterior distribution on the parameters, and samples a single value from that distribution. 

The main result (Theorem 1) is stated in terms of truncated concentrated DP, though I think it might be easier to state it in terms of Renyi DP (as a function of the $\alpha$ vector and the “order” $\lambda$) and then derive the tCDP and $(\epsilon,\delta)$-DP statements as corollaries. (I also think it would be worthwhile, in the corollaries, setting $\Delta_\infty$ and $\Delta_2$ to particular values, say 1, to get clear statements.) 

I have very few comments on the writing: the paper is clear and easy to understand and, I think, accurately represents and discusses the contributions. The only question here is significance: the paper is providing updated calculations for a well-know idea. It definitely should be published in an archival and refereed venue. I’m less sure that it meets the bar for NeurIPS. If the committee ultimately decides not to accept the paper, I would encourage the authors to submit to an appropriate journal.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Well addressed in a separate section.
